annotations.text: The differential diagnosis of the hepatic lesion is long, because the imaging characteristics are not clear-cut.
the loss of weight makes me consider the lesion a cancer until it has been proved otherwise.
if the lesion proved to be a locally advanced gastric lesion, the radiologic findings suggest that it could have been resected en bloc with the left lateral segment of the liver.

The stomach is not well defined on the CT scan, but the absence of a history of nausea, vomiting, or early satiety makes it unlikely that the mass was a gastric cancer involving the left lateral segment of the liver.
These characteristics are reminiscent of a hemangioma, perhaps a cavernous hemangiom
I therefore suspect that the lesion was not focal nodular hyperplasia.
Although metastases may be heterogeneous, they are not associated with rashes, except in the case of glucagonomas, which produce necrolytic migratory erythema.
As McFarland et al. have reported, hemangiomas and cystic lesions of the liver have a longer relaxation time than hepatic cancers.
Other benign lesions that should be considered in this case are focal nodular hyperplasia and hepatic adenoma.
The appearance is also not consistent with a pyogenic abscess, which may be multicentric or have a large central cavitating area.
The CT scan suggests the presence of a heterogeneous mass with a central area of necrosis or scarring and shows that the mass filled slowly in the arterial phase.
The lesion in this case could have been metastatic from another tumor, but if so, it should have been more hyperechoic on the ultrasonographic study.
Since the patient under discussion had no history of taking home remedies for her skin lesions, however, it is unlikely that she had been exposed to arsenic.
